claim: DeepSeek V4 achieves frontier-level performance (comparable to Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) with significantly lower cost due to superior algorithmic efficiency despite having fewer compute resources than US labs
actionable: Evaluate DeepSeek V4 for cost-sensitive production deployments where frontier performance is not required for all tasks

claim: Export controls on high-end GPU chips are not preventing frontier capability development because algorithmic innovations allow competitive models using constrained compute or less powerful chips
actionable: When making technology choices, assume that algorithmic advances will enable capable models despite hardware constraints

claim: Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture with 1.6T total parameters but only 49B active parameters allows running large models efficiently by activating only task-specific portions
actionable: Consider MoE architectures when you need frontier performance with constrained inference costs and latency requirements

claim: Distillation attacks at scale (150k exchanges from DeepSeek vs 3.4M from competitors) cannot explain quality differences, suggesting algorithmic advances rather than data theft drive performance
actionable: Do not overestimate the impact of small-scale distillation attacks on model capability - algorithmic innovations are likely the primary driver

claim: US enterprise adoption of Chinese open-source models creates dependency risks where sudden architectural changes or cutoffs could disrupt entire AI stacks built on those models
actionable: When choosing open-source models for production, evaluate geopolitical dependency risks and consider diversified model portfolios
